questions.stem: A point charge of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$ sits in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric field it produces at a point $0.30\ \text{m}$ away?
questions.explanation: The field of a point charge is $E = k\dfrac{Q}{r^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.30)^2} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{4}}{0.09} = 2.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. Leaving the distance unsquared gives $1.8 \times 10^{4}/0.30 = 6.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{N/C}$, near the $6.0 \times 10^{5}$ option after a power slip. The value $2.0 \times 10^{4}$ drops a factor of ten in the exponent. The trap is $6.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$, which mishandles the distance squared.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: What is the SI unit of electric field strength, and what is an equivalent unit?
options: Newtons per coulomb (N/C), equivalent to joules per coulomb (J/C)
Coulombs per newton (C/N), equivalent to metres per volt (m/V)
Newtons per coulomb (N/C), equivalent to volts per metre (V/m)
questions.explanation: Electric field is force per unit charge, so its unit is the newton per coulomb. Because field is also the rate of change of potential with distance, $E = -\,dV/dx$, the same quantity equals volts per metre, and the two units are identical. Joules per coulomb is the volt, which measures potential, not field. The trap is the J/C option, which confuses electric field with electric potential.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A uniform electric field has magnitude $3.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. What is the magnitude of the force it exerts on a charge of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$ placed in it?
questions.explanation: The defining relation is $F = qE = (2 \times 10^{-6})(3.0 \times 10^{5}) = 0.60\ \text{N}$. The product of the exponents is $10^{-6} \times 10^{5} = 10^{-1}$, and $2 \times 3 = 6$, giving $6 \times 10^{-1} = 0.60$. Dividing the field by the charge instead of multiplying gives the huge $1.5 \times 10^{11}\ \text{N}$. A power-of-ten slip gives $6.0\ \text{N}$ or $0.060\ \text{N}$. The trap is $6.0\ \text{N}$, which loses one power of ten in the charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: At a certain point a $+5\ \mu\text{C}$ test charge feels an electric force of $0.30\ \text{N}$. What is the magnitude of the electric field at that point?
questions.explanation: Field is force per unit charge: $E = F/q = \dfrac{0.30}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{N/C}$. Dividing $0.30$ by $5$ gives $0.06$, and dividing by $10^{-6}$ raises it to $6.0 \times 10^{4}$. Multiplying force by charge instead gives the tiny $1.7 \times 10^{-5}$. A power slip gives $6.0 \times 10^{5}$. The trap is $1.5\ \text{N/C}$, which multiplies $0.30 \times 5$ and forgets the micro prefix entirely.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A point charge of $+5\ \mu\text{C}$ is in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric field it produces at a distance of $0.10\ \text{m}$?
questions.explanation: Apply $E = k\dfrac{Q}{r^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.10)^2} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{4}}{0.01} = 4.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/C}$. The key is $r^2 = 0.01$, dividing by which raises the result by $10^2$. Forgetting the square ($r = 0.10$) gives $4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. Over-squaring gives $4.5 \times 10^{7}$. The trap is $4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$, which uses $r$ instead of $r^2$ in the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: The direction of the electric field at a point is defined as the direction of the force on which kind of charge placed there?
questions.explanation: By convention the electric field points in the direction of the force experienced by a positive test charge. A negative charge feels a force opposite to the field, so using it would reverse the convention. A neutral particle feels no electric force, so it cannot define a direction. An electron is negative, so it would also point the wrong way. The trap is the negative test charge, whose force is antiparallel to the field by definition.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two charges produce electric fields at a point P. Charge A gives a field of $4.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$ pointing east, and charge B gives $3.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$ pointing north. What is the magnitude of the resultant field at P?
questions.explanation: Electric fields add as vectors. The two contributions are perpendicular, so the resultant is $E = \sqrt{(4.0)^2 + (3.0)^2} \times 10^{5} = \sqrt{25} \times 10^{5} = 5.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. Adding the magnitudes as if parallel gives $7.0 \times 10^{5}$, and subtracting gives $1.0 \times 10^{5}$. The value $2.5 \times 10^{11}$ stops at the sum of squares without taking the root. The trap is $7.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$, which adds perpendicular fields like ordinary numbers.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$ and $-2\ \mu\text{C}$ are placed $0.40\ \text{m}$ apart. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric field at the midpoint of the line joining them?
questions.explanation: At the midpoint each charge is $0.20\ \text{m}$ away, and each field has magnitude $E = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.20)^2} = 4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. The field of the $+$ charge points away from it (toward the $-$ charge), and the field of the $-$ charge points toward it (the same direction), so the two add: $E = 2 \times 4.5 \times 10^{5} = 9.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. They do not cancel, because for opposite charges the fields between them line up. The trap is $0\ \text{N/C}$, which wrongly assumes the fields oppose as they would for two like charges.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+3\ \mu\text{C}$ each are placed $0.40\ \text{m}$ apart. What is the magnitude of the electric field at the midpoint of the line joining them?
questions.explanation: Each charge is $0.20\ \text{m}$ from the midpoint, giving equal field magnitudes there. But the two fields point in opposite directions, each away from its own positive charge, so they cancel exactly and the net field is $0\ \text{N/C}$. This is the field null between two equal like charges. The individual magnitude $6.75 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$ is what each charge alone gives. The trap is $1.35 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/C}$, which adds the two equal fields instead of letting them cancel.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An electron (charge magnitude $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, mass $9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$) is placed in a uniform electric field of $1000\ \text{N/C}$. What is the magnitude of its acceleration? Give the answer to two significant figures.
questions.explanation: The force is $F = qE = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1000) = 1.6 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{N}$, and the acceleration is $a = F/m = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-16}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m/s}^2$. Such enormous accelerations are typical for electrons because their mass is so tiny. Quoting the force $1.6 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{N}$ as if it were the acceleration skips the divide by mass. The trap is $1.8 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m/s}^2$, which uses the proton mass in place of the electron mass.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two parallel plates are separated by $0.050\ \text{m}$ and have a potential difference of $100\ \text{V}$ across them. What is the magnitude of the uniform electric field between the plates?
questions.explanation: For a uniform field, $E = V/d = \dfrac{100}{0.050} = 2000\ \text{V/m}$. The smaller the gap, the stronger the field for a given voltage. Multiplying instead of dividing gives $5\ \text{V/m}$, and a power slip in the gap gives $200\ \text{V/m}$. The trap is $200\ \text{V/m}$, which uses $d = 0.50\ \text{m}$, a tenfold error in the plate spacing.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Electric field lines have several defining properties. Which statement is FALSE?
options: Two electric field lines can cross each other at a point in the field.
Field lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges.
The field is stronger where the lines are closer together.
The tangent to a field line gives the direction of the field there.
questions.explanation: Field lines never cross, because the field has a single well-defined direction at each point, and a crossing would assign two directions there. So the crossing statement is the false one and is the correct choice here. The other three are genuine properties: lines run from positive to negative charge, their density shows field strength, and their tangents give the field direction. The trap is reading the question as asking for a true statement; it asks for the false one.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge of $-3\ \mu\text{C}$ is placed in a uniform electric field of magnitude $5.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{N/C}$ that points east. What is the force on the charge?
questions.explanation: The magnitude is $F = |q|E = (3 \times 10^{-6})(5.0 \times 10^{4}) = 0.15\ \text{N}$. Because the charge is negative, the force points opposite to the field, so it is directed west rather than east. Getting the size right but keeping the eastward direction ignores the sign of the charge. The values $1.5\ \text{N}$ and $0.015\ \text{N}$ are power-of-ten slips. The trap is $0.15\ \text{N}$ east, which forgets that a negative charge is pushed against the field.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A point charge produces an electric field of magnitude $E$ at distance $r$. At distance $3r$ from the same charge, what is the field magnitude?
questions.explanation: The field of a point charge obeys $E \propto 1/r^2$. Tripling the distance multiplies $r^2$ by $9$, so the field falls to $E/9$. Treating it as $1/r$ gives $E/3$, which ignores the square. The field weakens with distance, so $3E$ is impossible. The trap is $E/3$, which forgets the inverse-square dependence and uses a simple inverse instead.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A proton (charge $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, mass $1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$) is released from rest in a uniform field of $300\ \text{N/C}$. What is the magnitude of its acceleration? Give the answer to two significant figures.
questions.explanation: The force is $F = qE = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(300) = 4.8 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{N}$, and $a = F/m = \dfrac{4.8 \times 10^{-17}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \approx 2.9 \times 10^{10}\ \text{m/s}^2$. Quoting the force $4.8 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{N}$ as the acceleration skips the mass. Using the electron mass would give the much larger $2.9 \times 10^{13}$. The trap is $2.9 \times 10^{13}\ \text{m/s}^2$, which divides by the electron mass rather than the proton mass.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Just outside the surface of a charged conductor, the electric field is always directed how, relative to the surface?
questions.explanation: On a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium any tangential field would push the free charges along the surface, but at equilibrium nothing moves, so the tangential component must be zero. That leaves the field perpendicular to the surface just outside. A parallel field would keep charges flowing, contradicting equilibrium. The field is not zero outside a charged conductor, only inside. The trap is the parallel option, which would prevent the charges from ever settling into equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Inside a hollow conductor that carries a static charge on its outer surface, with no charge enclosed in the cavity, what is the electric field?
options: Stronger near the centre of the cavity
Directed radially outward from the cavity centre
Equal to the field just outside the conductor
Zero everywhere inside the conductor material and the empty cavity
questions.explanation: Inside the material of a conductor in equilibrium the field is zero, since any field would drive the free charges until it vanished. With no charge inside the empty cavity, the field there is zero as well, the principle behind electrostatic shielding. The interior field is not equal to the exterior field, which is generally nonzero. There is no source inside the cavity to make the field grow toward the centre or point outward. The trap is equating the inside field with the outside field, ignoring that a conductor screens its interior.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A point charge of $+9\ \mu\text{C}$ sits at the origin. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, at what distance from the charge does the electric field magnitude equal $9000\ \text{N/C}$?
questions.explanation: Rearrange $E = kQ/r^2$ to $r = \sqrt{kQ/E}$. Here $kQ = 9 \times 10^{9} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 8.1 \times 10^{4}$, so $r^2 = \dfrac{8.1 \times 10^{4}}{9000} = 9$, giving $r = 3.0\ \text{m}$. Forgetting the square root and reporting $r^2 = 9$ as a distance gives $9.0\ \text{m}$. A power slip gives $0.30\ \text{m}$. The trap is $9.0\ \text{m}$, which quotes $r^2$ without taking the square root.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A tiny charged oil drop of mass $3.2 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{kg}$ is held motionless between two horizontal plates by a uniform electric field. Taking $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric force on the drop?
questions.explanation: For the drop to hang motionless the upward electric force must balance gravity: $F = mg = (3.2 \times 10^{-15})(10) = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{N}$. Multiplying by $g = 10$ raises the exponent by one, from $10^{-15}$ to $10^{-14}$. Dividing by $g$ instead gives $3.2 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{N}$, and forgetting $g$ altogether leaves the weight unmultiplied at $3.2 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{N}$. The trap is $3.2 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{N}$, which quotes the mass times one rather than mass times $g$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges, $+9\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0$ and $+1\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0.40\ \text{m}$, lie on the $x$-axis. At what point between them is the net electric field zero?
questions.explanation: The fields cancel where $\dfrac{9}{x^2} = \dfrac{1}{(0.40 - x)^2}$. Taking square roots, $\dfrac{3}{x} = \dfrac{1}{0.40 - x}$, so $3(0.40 - x) = x$, giving $1.2 = 4x$ and $x = 0.30\ \text{m}$. The null point lies closer to the smaller charge at $x = 0.40\ \text{m}$. The midpoint $0.20\ \text{m}$ ignores the unequal sizes, and $0.10\ \text{m}$ puts it near the large charge, which is backwards. The trap is $0.10\ \text{m}$, which solves the ratio in the wrong direction.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A point charge of $+5\ \mu\text{C}$ sits in vacuum. Take $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$. Find the magnitude of the electric field it produces at a point $0.10\ \text{m}$ away, and give a unit for the field.
questions.solution: The field of a point charge is $E = kQ/r^2$, so with distance it falls off as {{1}}. With $kQ = 4.5 \times 10^{4}$ in SI units and the separation squared in the denominator, the field magnitude is {{2}} $\times 10^{6}\ \text{N/C}$. Electric field can be quoted in newtons per coulomb or, equivalently, in {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A point charge gives $E = kQ/r^2$, an inverse-square field. Numerically $E = (9 \times 10^{9})(5 \times 10^{-6})/(0.10)^2 = (4.5 \times 10^{4})/0.01 = 4.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/C}$; using $r$ rather than $r^2$ would wrongly give $4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. Because field is also the rate of change of potential with distance, $\text{N/C}$ equals $\text{V/m}$. Joules per coulomb is the volt, a unit of potential rather than field, so it is the tempting wrong choice.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A uniform electric field of magnitude $3.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$ acts on a point charge of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$. Find the force on the charge and identify the correct relation between force, charge, and field.
questions.solution: The force a field exerts on a charge is given by {{1}}, so it grows in proportion to both the charge and the field. Substituting $q = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$ and $E = 3.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$, the force is {{2}} $\text{N}$. For a positive charge, this force points {{3}} the field.
questions.explanation: The defining relation is $F = qE$, so $F = (2 \times 10^{-6})(3.0 \times 10^{5}) = 6 \times 10^{-1} = 0.6\ \text{N}$, using $2 \times 3 = 6$ and $10^{-6} \times 10^{5} = 10^{-1}$. Dividing as $q/E$ or $E/q$ misplaces the quantities and wrecks the units. A positive charge is pushed along the field; a negative charge would be pushed opposite to it.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two parallel plates are $0.050\ \text{m}$ apart with a potential difference of $100\ \text{V}$ across them. Find the uniform field between the plates, and state how the field responds if the gap is reduced at fixed voltage.
questions.solution: For a uniform field between plates the relation is {{1}}. Substituting $V = 100\ \text{V}$ and $d = 0.050\ \text{m}$ gives a field of {{2}} $\text{V/m}$. Since the field is voltage divided by gap, reducing the gap at fixed voltage makes the field {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A uniform field satisfies $E = V/d = 100/0.050 = 2000\ \text{V/m}$. Multiplying as $Vd$ or inverting to $d/V$ gives the wrong size and units. Because $E$ is inversely proportional to $d$, squeezing the plates closer at the same voltage raises the field; a decimal slip to $d = 0.50\ \text{m}$ would give the too-small $200\ \text{V/m}$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An electron (charge magnitude $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, mass $9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$) sits in a uniform field of $1000\ \text{N/C}$. Find the magnitude of the force on it and its acceleration to two significant figures.
questions.solution: The electric force has magnitude $F = qE = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1000) = 1.6 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{N}$. To turn this force into an acceleration, divide it by {{1}}. Doing so gives an acceleration of about {{2}} $\times 10^{14}\ \text{m/s}^2$. This acceleration is enormous mainly because {{3}}.

options: the electron's mass is extremely small
the electron's charge is extremely large
gaps.answer: the electron's mass is extremely small

questions.explanation: Newton's second law gives $a = F/m$, so the force is divided by the electron's mass: $a = (1.6 \times 10^{-16})/(9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \approx 1.8 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m/s}^2$. Dividing by the charge or the field is dimensionally wrong, and using the proton mass ($1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$) would give a much smaller $1.1 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m/s}^2$. The acceleration is so large because the electron's mass is tiny, not because the field is unusually strong.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$ and $-2\ \mu\text{C}$ are $0.40\ \text{m}$ apart. Take $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$. Find the electric field magnitude at the midpoint of the line joining them.
questions.solution: At the midpoint each charge is $0.20\ \text{m}$ away, and each alone produces a field there of magnitude $4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. The field due to the positive charge points {{1}} it, while the field due to the negative charge points {{2}} it. Comparing those two directions at the midpoint, the contributions {{3}}, giving a net field magnitude of {{4}} $\times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$.
questions.explanation: A field points away from a positive charge and toward a negative one. At the midpoint, away-from the $+$ charge and toward the $-$ charge are the same direction, so the two equal fields add rather than cancel: $E = 2 \times 4.5 \times 10^{5} = 9.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$. The tempting error is to assume they cancel, which is what actually happens for two like charges, not opposite ones. Each charge alone gives $9 \times 10^{9} \times (2 \times 10^{-6})/(0.20)^2 = 4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/C}$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charged conductor sits in electrostatic equilibrium. State the electric field inside the body of the conductor, the direction of the field just outside its surface, and where the excess charge resides.
questions.solution: Inside the conducting material the free charges rearrange until they feel no net force, which forces the interior field to be {{1}}. Just outside the surface, any field component running along the surface would keep pushing charges sideways, but in equilibrium nothing moves; that component must vanish, leaving the external field pointing {{2}} the surface. For the same reason, the excess charge sits {{3}}.

options: equal to the surface charge density
uniform and parallel to the surface

questions.explanation: In equilibrium the free electrons move until the interior field is zero; any leftover field would keep driving them. A surviving tangential field just outside would likewise keep surface charges in motion, so the external field must be perpendicular to the surface. These same conditions force all the excess charge onto the outer surface, with none in the bulk or at the centre. The field is not zero just outside, only inside.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers
